title: High-Resistance Sensing
learningObjectives: Use very high resistance to limit current for sensing
Stabilize readings by minimizing current draw
Confirm circuits remain valid even with small currents
introText: Sensitive sensors draw micro-level currents. You'll push resistance high so current stays tiny, preserving voltage readings without loading the source.

steps.instructionText: Wire a 9V source and a resistor in a closed loop.
steps.feedbackOnSuccess: Circuit is valid and ready to measure.
steps.feedbackOnFailure: Connect both sides of the source and resistor to close the loop.

steps.instructionText: Increase the resistor until current falls below 1 mA.
steps.feedbackOnSuccess: Current is under 1 mA — excellent for high-impedance sensing.
steps.feedbackOnFailure: Raise the resistor value until current drops below 0.001 A.

steps.instructionText: Keep the loop intact and confirm the source still provides about 9V.
steps.feedbackOnSuccess: Voltage holds near 9V while current stays tiny — ideal for sensing.
steps.feedbackOnFailure: Set the source to roughly 9V and ensure wiring stays closed.

questions.questionText: Why use very high resistance when sensing voltage?
options: To avoid loading the source and changing the voltage
questions.explanation: High resistance keeps current tiny so the source voltage is not pulled down.

questions.questionText: If current must stay below 1 mA at 9V, what resistance range works?
questions.explanation: From I = V/R, keeping I < 0.001 A requires R > 9000Ω so current stays safely below 1 mA.
